clean_story: Sharp focus.  Mistakes shrink under pressure. "Expertise under pressure" means skills that stay precise when time collapses, like a surgeon’s hands steady during a bleeding crisis. It’s not about speed—it’s about automatic accuracy. Think of a pianist playing a concerto blindfolded:Their fingers don’t "think" about keys—they know from 10,000 hours where C-sharp hides in the dark. Pressure exposes gaps. A study of firefighters showed veterans made decisions 3x faster than rookies—not because they rushed, but because their brains recognized patterns instantly, like a chess master spotting checkmate in three moves. The mechanism? Myelin. Repeated practice wraps nerve fibers in this fatty sheath, turning slow signals into lightning. Under stress, myelinated pathways fire cleanly—while untrained ones short-circuit. Example:NASA’s Apollo 13 team improvised a CO₂ filter fix using only items on board—because their training drilled "what-if" scenarios until solutions became reflex. Three related subjects are skill automation, cognitive load limits, and failure resilience.
